content.text: Topology Is a Risk and Compliance Decision

Deployment topology determines which failure modes a system can survive, how quickly it recovers, where data is stored and processed, and which regulatory frameworks can be satisfied. These are not infrastructure details — they are fundamental constraints on the product. A system designed for a single-region topology cannot be promoted to production for a GDPR-regulated EU customer without architectural changes. Getting topology wrong at design time is expensive to fix; getting it wrong in production is a compliance or availability incident.

This topic defines four deployment topology templates for enterprise agentic AI systems, specifies the requirements each template satisfies, provides YAML topology specifications for the two most common production patterns, and includes a validation script for ensuring that a topology specification matches its stated requirements.

attrs.alt: Four deployment topology templates: Single-Region, Active-Active Multi-Region, Data-Sovereign, and On-Premises/VPC with their use cases and SLA characteristics
attrs.title: Four deployment topology templates: Single-Region, Active-Active Multi-Region, Data-Sovereign, and On-Premises/VPC with their use cases and SLA characteristics

content.text: GDPR, HIPAA, regulated industries with cross-border data restrictions

An active-passive topology maintains a standby region that only receives traffic after the primary fails. During recovery, the standby region must warm up (load models, re-establish connections, restore caches), which takes minutes and violates most enterprise SLA requirements. Active-active avoids this by keeping both regions continuously serving traffic: the secondary region is already warm when the primary fails, so failover is near-instantaneous.

The primary challenge of active-active is state synchronisation: if a user's session spans a region boundary (their first request goes to the US region and their second to the EU region), both regions must have a consistent view of session state. The standard solution is CRDTs (conflict-free replicated data types) for session state and event-driven propagation for configuration like prompt templates.

# deployment-topology-spec.yaml
# Topology: Active-Active Multi-Region for enterprise contract review AI

topology:
  id: active-active-multi-region
  version: "1.0"
  target_environment: production
  regions:
    primary: us-east-1
    secondary: eu-west-1

  global_routing:
    type: dns_weighted_failover
    provider: aws_route53
    health_check:
      endpoint: /health
      interval_seconds: 10
      failure_threshold: 2
      success_threshold: 1
    failover:
      primary_weight: 70    # 70% traffic to primary
      secondary_weight: 30  # 30% to secondary (always-on, not cold standby)
      automatic: true       # No manual intervention required for failover

  per_region:
    agent_fleet:
      runtime: kubernetes
      namespace: ai-agents
      min_replicas: 3
      max_replicas: 20
      autoscaler:
        metric: "model_gateway.request.queue_depth"
        target_value: 10   # Scale up when queue depth > 10 per replica
      pod_disruption_budget:
        min_available: 2   # Never go below 2 healthy replicas during rollout

    model_gateway:
      replicas: 2
      provider_routing:
        primary: anthropic_api   # claude-3-7-sonnet
        fallback: openai_api     # gpt-4o
      circuit_breaker:
        error_threshold: 5
        reset_timeout_seconds: 60

    memory_store:
      type: redis_cluster
      replication: cross_region  # Active-active Redis with CRDT
      ttl_working_memory_seconds: 3600
      ttl_session_seconds: 86400

    observability:
      tracing: aws_xray
      metrics: prometheus_remote_write → grafana_cloud
      logs: cloudwatch_logs

  state_sync:
    session_data:
      strategy: crdt_merge     # Conflict-free replicated data types
      sync_interval_ms: 500
    prompt_templates:
      strategy: event_driven   # Template updates propagate via SNS → SQS
    cost_attribution:
      strategy: async_batch    # Every 60 seconds; ordering not critical

  sla:
    availability: 0.9999  # 99.99% = 52 min/year downtime
    rto_seconds: 30       # Time to recover from region failure
    rpo_seconds: 5        # Maximum data loss on region failure
    p99_latency_ms: 3000  # End-to-end agent response

  data_residency:
    mode: preferred_region  # Data processed where user is; may replicate
    eu_users_restriction: false  # EU user data may replicate to US

  disaster_recovery:
    runbook: "https://wiki.internal/ai-platform/dr-runbook"
    quarterly_failover_drill: true
    recovery_validation:
      - "Agent responses match expected quality on canary prompts"
      - "No orphaned sessions detected after failover"
      - "Cost attribution complete for period spanning failover"

Data sovereignty requirements prohibit user data from being processed or stored outside the user's regulatory jurisdiction. For GDPR this means EU user data cannot be processed in US data centres. This requirement affects the entire stack: model API calls (which send user data to the LLM provider), storage, logging, and even observability metrics if they contain user-identifiable data.

The critical architectural insight is that the control plane and the data plane must be separated. The control plane (configuration, prompt template versions, anonymised metrics) can live in a single location. The data plane (user sessions, document content, model completions) must stay within the user's region. This separation allows central management without cross-border data transfer.

# deployment-topology-spec.yaml
# Topology: Data-Sovereign for GDPR compliance

topology:
  id: data-sovereign-eu-us
  version: "1.0"
  target_environment: production

  regions:
    - id: eu
      cloud_region: eu-west-1
      data_classification: GDPR_restricted
      users: ["EU", "EEA", "UK"]
    - id: us
      cloud_region: us-east-1
      data_classification: standard
      users: ["US", "CA", "APAC"]

  routing:
    type: geo_routing
    provider: aws_route53
    rules:
      - source_geo: ["EU", "EEA", "UK"]
        target_region: eu
        strict: true   # No fallback to US region for EU traffic
      - source_geo: ["*"]
        target_region: us

  data_governance:
    cross_region_transfer:
      eu_to_us: prohibited
      us_to_eu: prohibited
      shared_metadata:
        - "Anonymised usage metrics (no user ID)"
        - "Aggregated performance statistics"
        - "Prompt template versions (no user data)"
    data_at_rest:
      encryption: AES-256
      key_management:
        eu: aws_kms_eu_west_1
        us: aws_kms_us_east_1
      key_cross_region_access: prohibited

  control_plane:
    location: us-east-1  # Non-PII metadata only
    eu_data_visibility: none  # Control plane operators cannot see EU user data

  compliance:
    gdpr_dpa: "See DPA v3.1 in legal/contracts/vendor-agreements/"
    data_retention_days: 90
    right_to_erasure:
      endpoint: DELETE /v1/user/{user_id}/data
      propagation: both_regions
      sla_hours: 72
    privacy_impact_assessment: "docs/security/pia-2025-04.pdf"

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
from typing import Any


class TopologyType(Enum):
    SINGLE_REGION = "single_region"
    ACTIVE_ACTIVE = "active_active"
    DATA_SOVEREIGN = "data_sovereign"
    ON_PREMISES = "on_premises"


@dataclass
class TopologyRequirements:
    # Requirements that drive topology selection
    availability_sla: float            # e.g. 0.999, 0.9999, 0.99999
    rto_seconds: int                   # Recovery time objective
    rpo_seconds: int                   # Recovery point objective
    data_residency_regions: list[str]  # e.g. ["EU", "US"] or []
    regulatory_frameworks: list[str]   # e.g. ["GDPR", "HIPAA"]
    max_latency_p99_ms: int
    egress_allowed: bool               # False for air-gap requirements


def select_topology(reqs: TopologyRequirements) -> TopologyType:
    # Determine the appropriate topology based on requirements.
    # Returns the minimum topology that satisfies all requirements.

    # Air-gap requirement → on-premises
    if not reqs.egress_allowed:
        return TopologyType.ON_PREMISES

    # Strict data residency per region → data-sovereign
    if len(reqs.data_residency_regions) > 1:
        return TopologyType.DATA_SOVEREIGN

    # High availability + low RTO → active-active
    if reqs.availability_sla >= 0.9999 or reqs.rto_seconds <= 60:
        return TopologyType.ACTIVE_ACTIVE

    # Default for non-regulated, moderate availability
    return TopologyType.SINGLE_REGION


def validate_topology_against_requirements(
    topology: dict[str, Any],
    reqs: TopologyRequirements,
) -> list[str]:
    # Returns list of violations; empty list means topology satisfies requirements.
    violations = []

    topo_type = topology.get("topology", {}).get("id", "")
    sla_config = topology.get("topology", {}).get("sla", {})

    # Check availability SLA
    topo_availability = sla_config.get("availability", 0)
    if topo_availability < reqs.availability_sla:
        violations.append(
            f"Topology availability {topo_availability:.4f} < required {reqs.availability_sla:.4f}"
        )

    # Check RTO
    topo_rto = sla_config.get("rto_seconds", 3600)
    if topo_rto > reqs.rto_seconds:
        violations.append(
            f"Topology RTO {topo_rto}s > required {reqs.rto_seconds}s"
        )

    # Check RPO
    topo_rpo = sla_config.get("rpo_seconds", 3600)
    if topo_rpo > reqs.rpo_seconds:
        violations.append(
            f"Topology RPO {topo_rpo}s > required {reqs.rpo_seconds}s"
        )

    # Check data residency for data-sovereign topologies
    if reqs.data_residency_regions:
        governance = topology.get("topology", {}).get("data_governance", {})
        cross_region = governance.get("cross_region_transfer", {})
        for region in reqs.data_residency_regions:
            key = f"{region.lower()}_to_us"
            if cross_region.get(key) != "prohibited":
                violations.append(
                    f"Data residency required for {region} but cross-region transfer not prohibited"
                )

    # Check p99 latency
    topo_latency = sla_config.get("p99_latency_ms", 10000)
    if topo_latency > reqs.max_latency_p99_ms:
        violations.append(
            f"Topology p99 latency {topo_latency}ms > required {reqs.max_latency_p99_ms}ms"
        )

    return violations


def generate_dr_runbook_outline(topology: dict[str, Any]) -> str:
    topo = topology.get("topology", {})
    regions = topo.get("regions", {})
    sla = topo.get("sla", {})

    lines = [
        f"# Disaster Recovery Runbook: {topo.get('id', 'unknown')}",
        "",
        "## 1. Detection",
        f"- Health check failure threshold: {topo.get('global_routing', {}).get('health_check', {}).get('failure_threshold', 2)} consecutive failures",
        f"- Automatic failover: {topo.get('global_routing', {}).get('failover', {}).get('automatic', False)}",
        "",
        "## 2. Failover Procedure",
        "- Confirm health check failures are not transient (check CloudWatch alarms)",
        "- If automatic failover is disabled: update Route 53 weight to 0 for failed region",
        "- Alert on-call engineer via PagerDuty",
        "",
        "## 3. Recovery Validation",
    ]

    dr = topo.get("disaster_recovery", {})
    for check in dr.get("recovery_validation", []):
        lines.append(f"- {check}")

    lines.extend([
        "",
        f"## 4. SLA Impact",
        f"- RTO target: {sla.get('rto_seconds', 'N/A')}s",
        f"- RPO target: {sla.get('rpo_seconds', 'N/A')}s",
        "",
        "## 5. Post-Incident",
        "- Complete incident report within 48 hours",
        "- Run failover drill within 30 days to validate recovery",
    ])

    return "
".join(lines)

In multi-region topologies, cross-region network calls add measurable latency. A synchronous call from EU to US adds approximately 80–100ms round-trip time. For agent orchestration patterns that make multiple model calls per user turn, even small added latencies compound. Design the agent's execution paths so that cross-region calls occur only for background state synchronisation, not on the critical path of user-visible responses.
Keep all synchronous agent-to-model calls within the same region as the user's request
Cross-region state synchronisation should be asynchronous and off the critical response path
Cache aggressively within the regional deployment to reduce intra-region latency
Use read replicas for session state in the secondary region to avoid cross-region reads
Set regional latency budgets explicitly: if the US region adds > 200ms to EU responses, the routing is wrong

Enterprise deployments often serve multiple tenants from a shared infrastructure. Within a single topology, tenant isolation must be enforced at the network, compute, and data layers. Network isolation via VPC/VLAN segmentation per tenant or per tenant tier. Compute isolation via dedicated Kubernetes namespaces or dedicated node pools for tenants with strict isolation requirements. Data isolation via separate Redis key namespaces, database schemas, or physical databases for regulated tenants.
Classify tenants by isolation tier: shared compute, shared infra / isolated namespace, or dedicated infra
Tenants with regulated data (HIPAA BAA, SOC 2 Type II requirement) must be on isolated compute
Shared compute can coexist with isolated data: use namespace prefixes and RBAC to prevent data leakage
Document tenant isolation tier in the service agreement and in the deployment topology spec
Test isolation: run a penetration test that specifically attempts cross-tenant data access after each major deployment

Changing topology from single-region to active-active is not a configuration change — it is a migration that requires schema changes, data synchronisation setup, DNS changes, and load testing at the new traffic distribution. Plan topology migrations with a feature flag that routes a small percentage of traffic to the new topology, validates behaviour, then increments traffic. Never switch 100% of traffic to a new topology without a tested rollback path.
Topology selection is driven by availability SLA, RTO, RPO, data residency requirements, and regulatory frameworks — not by preference or familiarity.
Active-active multi-region is the production standard for customer-facing products: it achieves 99.99% availability and near-zero RTO by keeping all regions warm and synchronised.

Data-sovereign topology separates the control plane (central) from the data plane (regional): user data never crosses the regional boundary, but configuration and anonymised metrics can be managed centrally.
Validate topology specifications programmatically: check that the declared availability, RTO, RPO, and data governance policies satisfy the stated requirements before deployment.
Topology migrations require staged rollouts with feature flags, not big-bang cutovers: validate each traffic increment before proceeding to the next.
